inclusive_learning_strategies: Introduction to the Integrated Unit: Language Arts related to the topic: Discussion or Guest Speaker:
Question for the week: How do plants and animals make a difference to me?

The teacher may start the unit by asking the learners what they like to eat. (Many other options: the teacher may bring in some fruits, vegetables. Or, if food is served at the school, the teacher may take the children to the kitchen to see what is cooked there; or invite the cook to the class to talk about what is cooked in the school kitchen. Or the teacher may invite a local community person who grows a small patch of vegetables to come in to chat with the children).

When the teacher plans for a guest to come into the classroom, or to take the children on a little “field trip” within the school, the teacher will prepare the children before the event. The teacher will explain what will be happening and encourage the children to think about what they might learn and what questions they might have. During the visit, the teacher will support the guest to provide age-appropriate information to the children and to respond to the children’s questions. After the visit, the teacher will encourage the children to write/draw a response to what they learned (please see the form, page 11, in this document).
Discussion: As the teacher and children discuss what they like to eat (or what foods they saw or heard about above), the teacher may ask “where does the food come from”?
Language Arts and Science- Class Scrapbook and/or Individual Scrapbooks about Living and Non-Living Things

Group Work: To complete this activity the teacher will gather all materials needed such as pictures (that parents can assist with in advance - glue, marker/ pencils, construction paper cut -out letters/words etc. Learners will be divided into small groups where they will sort pictures into living and non- living things. (Encourage discussion to ascertain their choices). Each group/ individual within groups will work on decorating their scrapbook to look visually appealing. The teacher will move around and assist the learners accordingly.
Partner Writing: Learners will then be guided to label the pictures with the appropriate words, using letter sounds to sound out words.

The teacher must explain that a living thing can eat and breathe, has young, and can grow. Non-living things do not do those things.

inclusive_learning_strategies: The children may answer “from the market!”, and the teacher may wonder, “how does that food get to the market?” Eventually, a child (or the teacher) may say that the chickens are grown on a farm, the eggs for the cake come from the chickens, the milk for the ice cream come from the cow, or nuts etc. The teacher will sing a version of Old MacDonald Had A Farm with the children, filling in the kinds of animals and plants that would be farmed locally. While singing the song, the teacher would pause and ask, “Why does Old MacDonald raise cows, ducks, sheep, banana trees, dasheen, mango? Etc.”

Then, the teacher should ask the question How Do Plants and Animals Make a Difference to Me and My World? (and create a chart with the learners’ answers). The children would offer some information about providing food, and then the teacher will probe more, such as asking if there is a plant that helps them to find some shade when it’s hot. This chart could be displayed in the classroom, and the teacher and children could add to the chart throughout the 5 weeks of the IU. While the teacher is creating the chart, some “incidental” teaching of letters/sounds/sight words will occur. (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions to enhance Shared Writing and Shared Reading experiences). After the chart is completed, the children may take part in Shared Reading. The teacher may add items to the chart as the 5 weeks of the Integrated Unit unfold.

animals) and non-living things (chair, rock, book), two separate containers labeled “Living” and “Non-Living.” Each learner will be given a picture to decide if it's living or non-living by placing the picture into one of the containers. Language Arts and Science - Nature Walk
Which living things are plants and which living things are animals?
Help the learners distinguish between living things that are plants and living things that are animals. This can be done by using songs like "Old MacDonald Had a Farm" to introduce vocabulary and basic information about different animals and plants. The teacher will use plants and animals present on local farms to insert while singing the song. (pigs, goats, sheep, rabbits, carrots, dasheen, potatoes, banana, tomatoes, lettuce, cabbage, mango, oranges, melon) etc. The teacher can help the children to say which things are plants and which things are animals.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M3WvViAUgyg
Or, instead of Old MacDonald, the teacher may use the song ‘Looby Loo’. (I put my plants in, I put my animals out, etc.).
Which living things are plants?
Take learners for a walk in the environment to see living and nonliving things and to look for living things that we call “plants”.
Have learners sing the tune “Here we go around the Mulberry Bush”, as they skip around, they would name the plants and any non- living things that are seen in the environment.

Have learners take a plant (or a leaf from a plant) back into the classroom (where plants are available) teacher would guide the learners on what plant to choose. (Where plants are not available to take back, teacher provides),
Pair Groups
Each learner will work with another learner to observe the plant or leaf they brought back to class or the one that is provided. The teacher offers support using through questioning (“What color is the plant?” What shape? Any veins? Any texture? Etc. They will talk about and give answers to the questions What is a plant? Learners’ answers will be recorded. The teacher would lead learners to a definition. (Plants are living things that grow by making their own food using sunshine and water.)
Play Dough Modeling
Learners will observe the plant or leaf and create a model with play dough. Or the children may use paper and scissors to create their plants. Teacher may want to give them coloured paper and model how to cut to create roots etc

Language Arts and Science - What is an animal?
Learners will observe and talk about the different animals displayed (via pictures, screen, real life animals etc.) by the teacher. They will give answers to the question What is an animal? Learners’ answers will be recorded. The teacher would lead learners to a definition. (Animals are living things that eat other plants or animals for food, in order to live.)
Why do animals hunt?
Why don’t plants hunt?
What are plants?
What are animals?
Compare characteristics of plants and animals.

Developing Topic- Group Work
Learners will sit in groups. Each group will be given a mystery bag. (E.g. leaves- mint, tea bush, feather, fruit, egg, toy animal etc.) Where learners can pull different objects relating to plants and animals and have a chit chat surrounding how plants and animals make a difference to them. The teacher listens to their ideas and experiences.

Suggested questions to ask
-What do we use these leaves for?
-Where do you think these feathers come from and what do we use them for?
-Why do we eat fruits?
-Why do you have eggs at home?

The teacher could add some new ideas to the chart made at the beginning of the unit, about how plants and animals make a difference to us.

Have learners sing and do actions to the song Head shoulder, knees and toes or Chest nut toe, knee, noses, eye, love Him.

Lead learners into a conversation about identifying similarities between body parts of animals and body parts of humans using learners standing and touching their body parts. The body parts are recorded.

Some incidental teaching of letters/sounds will be done as the teacher records children’s answers (Shared Writing; please see Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for further suggestions).

then humans are animals

Introduction to Week Two: Language Arts and Science: What do plants and animals need to survive? What does 'survive' mean?
Teachers may start the week by providing multiple scenarios (video clips) to cement the meaning of the concept 'survive'
Scenario 1: Natural Disasters- Hurricanes or earthquake or Tsunami, After viewing video clippings the teacher leads the learners to how people survive natural and other disasters.

Scenario 2: Diseases, fires https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GHjgwqj62E (what is a story board?) With the use story board the teacher tells: - the story of how someone went through the Covid-19 pandemic and how they survived. - the story of a fire that took place and how persons survive
Possible definitions of the concept 'survive' -I must live and be healthy -To be alive -To have what we need to live -There are things around us that can hurt us but if we stay safe, we stay alive.
The children may be reminded of the motto they learned during the Integrated Unit on Weather: Be Prepared: Not Scared.
Learners view a video that captures humans and animals need for water. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kzhdLvakY7g

Suggested Questions to guide discussion 1.Why were animals moving away? 2. Why were the animals dying? 3. 'If there is no water, there is no life' What does this mean? 4. What happened to the plants? 5. What do animals need to survive?

Language Arts and Science: What do plants need to survive? The teacher tells the learners that they are going to start a science experiment to see how plants survive! Explain that two plant homes will be set up and they will be observing which plant is surviving and why.
Modeling I Listen I do I say

Language Arts and Science: Obstacle Course and Poem
Poem
The teacher displays the poem on a chart and has learners repeat with actions
Plants don't move around, that's the key (Stand still with arms at side)
They make their own food, you see! (clap clap)
Light, water, air they take (Reach arms up high, sip, breath out fast)
Healthy growth, for goodness’ sake! (Stomp both feet with a smile)
The children will draw a picture of the plant that survived the experiment. This picture will provide examples of the child’s letter formation and spelling of name.
Learners will draw and color the plants that survived. They will write their names on their paper and the sentence- “This plant survived!”
Language Arts, Math and Science: Plant Shop
Plant Shop

This ILS provides many opportunities for the teacher to enhance the children’s development of LA skills through Shared Reading. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions.

Set up a pretend plant shop with different sections labeled "Sun-loving Plants" (cacti, aloes, hibiscus...) and "Shade-loving Plants" (ferns, Hosta...). Real plants are best but in cases where they are not available use pictures or toy plants.
The teacher dresses and acts like a florist, explaining to the learners what is needed by all plants and what some plants need, and others may not need to survive.
Learners are given opportunities to ask questions and make comments.

The teacher points learners’ attention to the signs in the shop (Sun loving plants, Shade loving plants) and guides the learners to read the signs by decoding the words. Learners will also study a chart that the teacher has made, with a list of items and cost. The chart will include items a plant will need to survive, such as water, shade, sunlight, soil nutrients).
Learners will work in pairs to identify a plant and "buy" the items they think their plant needs (sunlight, water, shade, air, soil nutrients, etc.). The teacher clarifies accordingly.

In reading the chart and buying the items at the plant shop, learners will name monies and identify quantities in cents. (E.g. 1 shade cost 5 cents and 1 sunlight costs 3 cents then 5 cents and 3 cents make 8 cents; 5+3=8). They will also count objects. This should all be done orally, with the teacher’s support.

The teacher will invite children to take part in role play with the Plant Shop at various times, when the children have the opportunity to choose various activities (such as drawing, puzzles, building).

Language Arts and Science: Animals Need Food: Videos and Discussion
Review the video of the frog eating bugs (see ILS in Week One), question learners on what the frog (and the eagle) are doing to stay alive/survive.
Write responses on the board.
Why is the frog hunting the insects?
Why is the eagle hunting the frog?
Why do animals need food?
What other things do animals need to survive/live? (Food, water, shelter, air).
Revisit the story of The Hungry Caterpillar. Learners will name the different foods that the caterpillar ate.
The teacher and the children will continue to think about different animals and they will consider if each animal gets food from animals or from plants or from both animals and plants.

Language Arts, Science: Plants and Animals depend on each other
Learners will sing along with the song: “The Green Grass grows round and round..” And The Green Grass Grew All Around | + More Kids Songs | Super Simple Songs (youtube.com)

After watching the video and singing along, the teacher will make a list (with the help of the children) of each element in the song and draw arrows between the elements. The class will watch the video again, and stop to give the teacher the chance to do the writing - to make sure the list is complete!
Green grass – hole – tree – branch - twig– nest – bird - egg

The teacher will explain that plants and animals depend on their habitat, but they also depend on each other. So in the song, the bird depended on the tree to have twigs, and a safe space to have a nest.
Teacher Led Discussion: The teacher will explain to the learners how plants and animals depend on each other and the environment where they live

Plants Give Animals Food: "Plants, like trees and flowers, grow in the ground. They make food from sunlight, water, and the air. Some animals, like birds, rabbits, and even some bugs, eat the leaves, fruits, and seeds that plants make. Can you imagine a rabbit munching on a carrot? That's a plant helping an animal!"
Language Arts, Science: Plants and Animals live in an “environment”
The teacher can remind the children what they have learned about plants and animals depending on each other. The plants and animals who live in the same place make up an “environment”.
Plants and animals need each other:

The teacher may show this video to the children, stopping several times to write on a chart, so everyone will remember what they are learning. For example, stop the video and write: “Animals depend on plants for food”, or “Animals depend on plants for shelter”, etc.

Language Arts and Science: Making a model of a habitat
To review, the teacher poses the question ‘Why do you think plants and animals live where they do?’
Responses are recorded on a concept map completed by the teacher.
Making a model: Pair work

Learners will work with a partner over several days to make a model of a habitat: one place where plants and animals (humans) live that meets their needs. The partners will need to agree which habitat they will create and which animals and plants they will put in their habitat. The children will create their model on a piece of cardboard, using play dough, construction paper, etc.

The teacher will assist the children by asking them what challenges they are having, and if they have found some good solutions. The teacher will call all the children together to discuss challenges and solutions, and then invite the children to work some more on their model.
Introduction to Week Four: Language Arts and Science: How do plants change the environment?

Setting the Scene: The teacher will tell the learners to imagine a vibrant forest. Birds are chirping, leaves are rustling, and flowers are blooming. The forest is full of life and activity. Every plant and animal in this forest plays a special role in making the environment the way it is. The teacher poses the question: Can plants and animals change the place where they live? (Environment)

The teacher probe learners to respond and waits. The teacher may ask who says “no” and who says “yes”. Count the number of children.) Ask the children why they say “yes” – what are they thinking about? Why do they say “no” – what are they thinking about? The teacher will let the children know how important their thinking is. Also, that as we learn more, they may change their thinking, and that’s what learning is all about.
The teacher will tell the children they are going to watch a video that may change their ideas about how plants can change the environment.
Language Arts, Math, and Science: Plant Hero Adventure Game
Learners will learn how various plants change their environment by acting as "Plant Heroes". The children will complete 3 different missions related to the plant heroes.
The teacher sets up three (3) different stations in the classroom or the school environment and will divide the children into three groups.

Each station will have a plant (or picture of a plant in the absence of a real plant), a card with information about the plant in relation to how it changes the environment and a mission/ activity to complete. The teacher will show all the children the plants and will read to all the children what the plant does. The teacher will also explain what the children should do on the worksheets at the stations.

Learners will start in their groups at a particular station. They will look at the plant/picture, read (teacher may read) and discuss how it changes the environment and then complete the provided activity. At the sound of a whistle they will move from one station to the next until all stations are completed.

Teachers can choose any plant within context and also any number of stations according to the number of learners in class. Stations can be given special names for interest and motivation. Please see additional information in the Useful Content for Teachers section below.
The teacher may ask additional adults to help during this ILS.

After listening to the poem, the teacher questions learners as to what we refer to as a house. Have the learners give a short description of where they live. What kinds of things do the children do at their house? Do they get the things they need to survive (refer back to discussions in Week Two: food, water, shelter)

The teacher may repeat the poem, as a Shared Reading experience, pointing out high frequency words, letters/sounds, rhyming words, etc. (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies or further tips to make a Shared Reading experience as effective as possible).

(This is a Shared Writing experience: please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for further ideas to make this experience helpful for developing children’s skills in Language Arts). As a response to the story and discussion, the children will draw a picture of one place that the polar bears thought about living, but that place would not be good for them. The children will complete a sentence under their picture, “I can’t live here.” The teacher will ask each child, while they are working, why the polar bear could not live there. The teacher will ask the children to create a diagram to show one plant or animal depending on another plant or animal. The teacher will show the children how to draw an arrow between the plant and the animal to show that they are connected. For example, the child might draw a tree and a bird, and put an arrow between the tree and the bird to show that the bird depends on the tree. The child will be asked to label the diagram, and so will print the words “tree” and “bird” (for example), with support from the teacher, if necessary.

Video: Play -Pause -Share: Learners will view a video. The teacher will stop the video at various parts, inviting questions and posing questions, to bring out the various ways that plants can change their environment. Below are suggested times to pause.

Q and A session (0.6.0- 0.14 s) Q- what do we mean when we say the environment? A- Everything around a living thing (Question to find out if they remember what is a living thing)
(0.15-0.22 s) Q- How can tree roots change their environment? A- They can grow strong and even break apart concrete.
(0.23-0.38 s) Q- What about the branches and leaves, how can they change the environment? A- They provide shade that keeps the ground cool and provide a home for some animals.
(0.50-1.24 s) Q- How can a fallen tree change the environment? A- It can become a home to small animals (Review habitat)

inclusive_learning_strategies: Introduction to Week Two: Language Arts and Science: What do plants and animals need to survive? What does 'survive' mean?
Teachers may start the week by providing multiple scenarios (video clips) to cement the meaning of the concept 'survive'
Scenario 1: Natural Disasters- Hurricanes or earthquake or Tsunami, After viewing video clippings the teacher leads the learners to how people survive natural and other disasters.

Scenario 2: Diseases, fires https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GHjgwqj62E (what is a story board?) With the use story board the teacher tells: - the story of how someone went through the Covid-19 pandemic and how they survived. - the story of a fire that took place and how persons survive
Possible definitions of the concept 'survive' -I must live and be healthy -To be alive -To have what we need to live -There are things around us that can hurt us but if we stay safe, we stay alive.
The children may be reminded of the motto they learned during the Integrated Unit on Weather: Be Prepared: Not Scared.
Learners view a video that captures humans and animals need for water. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=kzhdLvakY7g

Language Arts and Science: What do plants need to survive? The teacher tells the learners that they are going to start a science experiment to see how plants survive! Explain that two plant homes will be set up and they will be observing which plant is surviving and why.
Modeling I Listen I do I say

Setting Up Homes
Cup#1: Explain that in one cup, there will be a healthy plant and give it all the things we think a plant will need to grow strong. Ask the learners to guess what those things will be. (Wait for responses like sunlight, water, air)
Explain that the plant will be placed near the window for lots of sunlight. We’ll also make sure to give it water regularly and keep it in an open area where it can get fresh air.
Cup#2 Explain that in the other cup, there will be a plant, but this time, we won’t give it any sunlight, water, or fresh air. We’ll keep it in a closed space away from the window.

Observation and Prediction:
What do you think will happen to the plants over the next few days? Which plant do you think will survive better? Why? (Encourage discussion and guesses)
Tell the learners to check every day on the plants and think about what they observe.
(These experiments would be observed over the next two days.)
After two days of observation and data collection there will be a grand conversation. Learners will be encouraged to ask questions about the experiment. The teacher will then pose the following questions.

Observation Corner- Who will survive?
What did you notice over the two days you looked at the plants?
How do the plants look?
Why are they looking like that?
Which one survived?
Why did the other one die or shriveled up?
What do plants need to survive?
Learners will draw and color the plants that survived. They will write their names on their paper and the sentence- “This plant survived!”

Obstacle Course
Set up an obstacle course inside or outside the classroom where learners must complete tasks related to plant needs:
1. crawl under a tunnel for "soil," (this is what the plants’ roots do)
2. reach for a toy sun for "sunlight" (this is what the plants’ stems and leaves do)
3. then run with a jug for “water” (this is what the plants’ stems do)
Language Arts, Math and Science: Plant Shop
Plant Shop

This ILS provides many opportunities for the teacher to enhance the children’s development of LA skills through Shared Reading. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions.

Set up a pretend plant shop with different sections labeled "Sun-loving Plants" (cacti, aloes, hibiscus...) and "Shade-loving Plants" (ferns, Hosta...). Real plants are best but in cases where they are not available use pictures or toy plants.
The teacher dresses and acts like a florist, explaining to the learners what is needed by all plants and what some plants need, and others may not need to survive.
Learners are given opportunities to ask questions and make comments.

The teacher points learners’ attention to the signs in the shop (Sun loving plants, Shade loving plants) and guides the learners to read the signs by decoding the words. Learners will also study a chart that the teacher has made, with a list of items and cost. The chart will include items a plant will need to survive, such as water, shade, sunlight, soil nutrients).
Learners will work in pairs to identify a plant and "buy" the items they think their plant needs (sunlight, water, shade, air, soil nutrients, etc.). The teacher clarifies accordingly.

In reading the chart and buying the items at the plant shop, learners will name monies and identify quantities in cents. (E.g. 1 shade cost 5 cents and 1 sunlight costs 3 cents then 5 cents and 3 cents make 8 cents; 5+3=8). They will also count objects. This should all be done orally, with the teacher’s support.

The teacher will invite children to take part in role play with the Plant Shop at various times, when the children have the opportunity to choose various activities (such as drawing, puzzles, building).

Language Arts and Science: Where can I live?
Read Aloud – Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies to enhance the development of many Language Arts skills through this Read Aloud experience.
Learners will listen to a story I Can't Live Here https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zt1jvQUaI4w
Guided Questions
1. Why was the little polar bear sad? ( His mother said they have to find a new home)
2. Where did they stop first? (forest)
3. Why was the forest not suitable for a little polar bear? (The stream was not deep enough to swim)
4. What was the little polar bear’s concern about living in the desert? (It was too hot)
5. Why could the Polar bears could not live at the lake? (They would not find the right kind of fish to eat.)
6. Where did they eventually live and why?

Plants Give Animals Homes: "Plants also give animals places to live. Birds build their nests in trees, and bees make their hives in flowers. Even big animals like bears will live near lots of plants in a forest. So, plants are like homes for many animals!"

Animals Help Plants: "Animals help plants too! When bees visit flowers to get nectar, they carry pollen from one flower to another. This helps the flowers make seeds and grow new plants. And when animals eat fruits, they spread the seeds around, which helps new plants grow in different places."

Environment Is Important: "Plants and animals live in different environments like forests, ponds, and mountains. These environments give them everything they need to live. Plants need sunlight, water, and soil. Animals need food, water, and a safe place to live. If we take care of the environment, we help plants and animals stay healthy and happy!"
The teacher will set up a “Web of Life” game for the children: https://www.amnh.org/explore/ology/biodiversity/web-of-life/activity-instructions

Or, the teacher will simplify the game by making cards (with picture and words). The children may sit in a circle and choose one card; say what is on the card, and tell what that plant or animal needs to survive. For example, one card will have a butterfly on it, and the child would say that the butterfly needs food from a plant; or another card will have a flower on it, and the child would say that the flower needs a bee to help move the pollen. The other children and the teacher could help each other to play this game.

The teacher will assist the children by asking them what challenges they are having, and if they have found some good solutions. The teacher will call all the children together to discuss challenges and solutions, and then invite the children to work some more on their model.
End of Week Three: Language Arts and Science: Presenting Models of Habitats
The teacher and children will prepare an invitation to send to parents/caregivers, other adults in the school, or another class of learners in the school.
Each pair of learners will stand by their model and will share information about it to the visitors. The visitors will circulate around the room to see all the models of the habitats.
The children will be answering the question for the week: Where do plants and animals live and why do they live there?
Language Arts, Math, and Science: Plant Hero Adventure Game
Learners will learn how various plants change their environment by acting as "Plant Heroes". The children will complete 3 different missions related to the plant heroes.
The teacher sets up three (3) different stations in the classroom or the school environment and will divide the children into three groups.

Each station will have a plant (or picture of a plant in the absence of a real plant), a card with information about the plant in relation to how it changes the environment and a mission/ activity to complete. The teacher will show all the children the plants and will read to all the children what the plant does. The teacher will also explain what the children should do on the worksheets at the stations.

Learners will start in their groups at a particular station. They will look at the plant/picture, read (teacher may read) and discuss how it changes the environment and then complete the provided activity. At the sound of a whistle they will move from one station to the next until all stations are completed.

Teachers can choose any plant within context and also any number of stations according to the number of learners in class. Stations can be given special names for interest and motivation. Please see additional information in the Useful Content for Teachers section below.
The teacher may ask additional adults to help during this ILS.

The teacher will write three sentences and ask for the children’s help with spelling the words Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for more ideas to make this an experience that helps the children to develop their skills in Language Arts.

Echo Reading: The teacher models fluency by reading aloud three sentences created from the science activity. After the reading learners will read with the same rate and prosody. 1. Coral vine keeps the soil from washing away. 2. Sea grapes have strong roots that keep the soil in place. 3. Banana plants grow big leaves that fall and make the soil rich.

Sight Words: As the teacher reviews how plants change the environment, the teacher will use some of these strategies to reinforce learning of the sight words. Prompt the learners to pay attention to the underlined words in each sentence. see and say- look at the word and say it, spell it- spell out the letters then call the word, Arm Tapping- Tap the letters on your arm and say the word, Air Writing- Write the word in the air, Book Writing- write the word in his/her book of Sight Words
The teacher might extend the discussion to the idea that some plants might have a harmful effect on the environment. For example, some plants might

Language Arts, Science: How do animals change the environment? The teacher asks questions to review what children learned about animals in previous weeks of this Integrated Unit, and to think about what they have now learned about how plants can change the environment. Where do animals live? How do animals move? Can plants change the place where they live? Are humans animals? Do animals change anything around them? The teacher waits for learners to respond after each question and asks if they have any questions to ask. Learners' answers and questions will be recorded.

Argument: The teacher makes the claim that just like plants change their environment, animals can change their environment too (Including humans). Today, we are going to explore how animals change their environment as we continue on our adventure to discover their amazing powers!

Group Work: The teacher provides learners with toys or pictures of different animals (e.g. earthworm, rabbit, ants, bird, caterpillar, bee, dog, horse, donkey, spider, jack Spaniard/wasp) and pictures of humans. They will work in small groups to: -Identify the pictures -talk about what they know about the animal/ human - talk about if that animal can change the environment

Shared Writing: The teacher will call all the children together. The teacher will hold up a picture, one at a time, of the animals the learners were discussing in their small groups. The teacher will ask how this animal will change their environment by. Learners will share and the teacher records information. The teacher may also add information that the children have not thought of. This is a Shared Writing activity. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions to enhance the children’s skills in Language Arts through activity.) The list that the teacher and children will create will include this information (and more, depending on all the pictures that the teacher provided to the small groups) o Horse/donkey - Eating plants/grass o Spider - Making web o Ants, Worm: Making tunnels or holes o Humans -Littering beaches o Humans – planting vegetables

Language Arts and Science: Video about animals changing their environment: The teacher and the children will engage in Shared Reading of the ways that Animals may change their environment, referring to the list that was prepared in the previous ILS. Learners will view a video that speaks to how animals change the environment. The learners will be asked to watch and listen carefully to see if there is information that is new, to add to what they already know. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XiywCGad ohQ
Role play: Learners would act out how each animal changes its environment using role-play or puppetry
Group Discussion: Engage the learners in a discussion by asking questions like: What did you learn about how animals change their environment?
Can you think of other ways animals/humans change where they live?
Why do you think animals / humans change their environment?

The children will be asked to draw a picture that shows one way that an animal can change the environment. It could be something that was learned from the video, or, it could be something that was mentioned previously with the children. The children will be supported to print one sentence under their drawing. Or, the teacher may prepare the paper for the children’s drawing, with space for the drawing and an incomplete (fill-in-the-blank) sentence at the bottom: “An animal can change the environment by __________.”
End of Week Four: Nature Walk/ Field Trip to Zoo, Animal Farm, Gardens
Take learners on a nature walk/Field Trip to observe animals in their habitats and how they change their environment.
Learners will draw a picture or share a story about their favorite animal (from nature walk or Field Trip) and how it changes its environment.

The teacher will lead the children in a discussion about the question for the week, Can plants and animals change the place where they live (the environment)? and will ask the children if they changed their mind about the answer they gave at the beginning of the week. Did they learn some new things this week?
The teacher may also do a review, as follows: Visual Summary/ Using our senses

Evidence clearly shows that plants and animals can change their environment. The teacher will use an infographic (Large pictures of a plant, an animal and a human) on the chalkboard/white board to summarize information using the guided questions.
Learners will be given sentence strips with information to stick on the graphic in answer to the questions.
1. What evidence did we see of how plants and animals/humans change the environment? (e.g., Bird droppings, garbage around us, leaves from trees, humans cut trees to build homes)
2. What did we hear that shows plants and animals / human change their environment (e.g., Dog barking, birds chirping, frogs croaking, rabbits dig holes)
3. What can we smell as changes are being made to the environment by plants and animals / human (e.g., garbage/materials burning, stench from littering)
Examples of sentence strips
Look! There is birds dropping on the floor.
Animals dig holes in the ground.
Seeds grow into trees.
The leaves are falling.
There is a smell of rubber in the air.
Humans cut down trees to build houses.

Introduction to Week Five: Language Arts, Mathematics, Social Studies and Science: Natural and Built Environments: The teacher will remind the children of the focus for Week Five of this Integrated Unit: how plants and animals may change their environment. The teacher will ask the children to tell ways that plants change the environment, and that animals change the environment. Link to the Social Studies SCOs (in the left column of this ILS) about natural and built environments and explain the terms “natural” and “built”. Ask the children to think about all the things around them in the school, out the window, etc. Which things are natural and which things are built by humans? The children will each draw and color an outdoor setting they are familiar with– e.g. the school, their house, the beach, the village. The teacher should prompt the child to add more things to their drawing (as appropriate). Then the children will label each item in their scene with “N” or “B” (N for natural; B for built). After, ask each child to count how many “N”s and how many “B”s they have in their scene. The teacher will make a chart with three columns: one column for children’s names, one column N (for Natural) and one column B (for Built). The teacher will ask each child to show his/her picture and count the N’s and the B’s, then add the information as the children count and report. For example, Joe will have 4 under the N and 5 under the B. The group will agree that there are quite a few “B”s, which shows that humans build (or change) many things in the environment. The teacher may ask the children why we, as humans, change the environment (to make shade, to make a smooth road, to make shelter, to make a place to sit, etc.). The teacher may explain that some of the changes that humans make to the environment are not good for the planet earth. Then, the teacher may tell the children the question for this week: Can we do some things so the environment is not changed so much by humans? The teacher may let the children provide answers to this question.

As a Shared Reading activity, the teacher will go over the words of the song, emphasizing the rhyming words, letter sounds, high frequency/sight words (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for specific ideas to enhance the Language Arts skills and knowledge).
The teacher should also help the children understand what the song means – why do we want the planet to be green and blue? What does “saving the planet” mean?
Then the teacher will play the first part of the video (below) and invite the children to sing the chorus, while the teacher points to the words on the chart.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=57MpZ-1VmRs We are Green Champions

Have the child watch this video, stop at specific times, and the teacher write a list of what children can do (planting a tree, recycling, saving electricity, saving water). After the video, the teacher will go over the list with the children, and ask them what they can do to help the planet. Can they do any of the things listed in the video?

Note: there is Shared Writing and Shared Reading in the last parts of this ILS. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions of ways to incorporate Language Arts skills during this experience.

Language Arts and Science: What can we do? The teacher asks the children: Do you think we as human beings need to help Earth survive? What can we do to help Earth survive? How do we depend on the healthy earth?
Read Aloud (or Video) “The Earth Book” by Todd Parr. https://youtu.be/lpEc5nHqO2c?si=hCiFV24ZUmA633S

Gather the children in a circle. Hold up "The Earth Book" by Todd Parr or similar book that speaks to how humans can help to take care of the earth. This is a Shared Reading. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for specific ideas of ways to make the most of a Read Aloud. Watch for ideas of what to do before, during and after this specific book! After reading the book, discuss the different ways the characters in the book helped the Earth. Ask the children which ideas they liked best and why.

Write their responses on a large chart paper or whiteboard. Check back to the end of the video to see if the children remembered all the 9 ideas listed. (Make the most of this Shared Writing experience by looking for suggestions in the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies. Read through the list with the children, to make it a Shared Reading experience as well). Introduce the children to the concepts of reuse, reduce and recycle

Role Play Learners will work in groups as “Helpers” to help Earth by acting out different things that we as humans can do to help (e.g., picking up trash, saving water, planting trees – refer to the things on the list that the teacher made after watching the video. Teacher gives simple props to each group to help them get into character (e.g., cardboard trash, toy watering cans).

Charades The teacher must choose one child and whisper one way to help the earth (pick up trash), then the child may act out that way to help, without speaking, and the other children will guess what the child is doing.
Language Arts, Mathematics, and Science: Recycling Class Project Teacher, parents and learners source bottles, materials for collage and seeds etc.
Sorting Recyclables: Bring out a collection of recyclable and non-recyclable items. Have learners sort them into the correct bins (use pictures or color-coded bins for guidance).

Math Data Collection: Counting and Charting: Once all items are sorted, gather the class around the chart paper or whiteboard. Count the number of items in the recyclable bin together, writing the total number on the board under a "Recyclable Items" heading. Repeat the process for the non-recyclable bin, writing the total number under a "Non-Recyclable Items" heading. Ask the children to compare the two quantities: Which bin has more items? Which bin has fewer items? The teacher can emphasize how we can turn something old into something useful.
The teacher should show the learners how to make a bird feeder from a plastic bottle: https://www.pbs.org/parents/crafts-and-experiments/how-to-make-a-plastic-bottle-birdfeeder
The class may make a bird feeder as a group, and hang it in the school yard.

The teacher should ask the children to bring in used buttons, bottle caps, pull tabs, bread tags, small twigs, seeds, etc. The children will sort the items into containers. The teacher will model how to make a pattern with the two kinds of items: e.g. bottle cap, seed, bottle cap, seed, bottle cap seed, and then will ask the children to make their own pattern, or to extend the teacher’s pattern.

The teacher should pull together some photos from the internet, to show children examples of art made with “beautiful junk”. Using a piece of recycled cardboard as a base, the children will create beautiful designs and pictures with the recyclable materials. They will print their name on the cardboard, and perhaps a title for their design. Note: it is very challenging to glue the items onto a piece of paper. So the teacher may take photographs of the children’s designs/pictures, and then the items may be recycled.

End of Week Five: End of Integrated Unit: Language Arts and Science: Celebration! The teacher may invite the families or other adults in the school, or another class in the school to visit the room and to help with celebrating all the learning from the Unit. Create a class poster with drawings and handprints with the title "Our Promise to Mr. Earth” to show how the children will help the environment. Dear children, gather close, come near, There's something special you must hear. A heartfelt message just for you, For all the kind things that you do. You pick up trash, you save the trees, You care for animals, birds, and bees. You turn off pipes, you plant new seeds, You do such good, helpful deeds. Your little hands, your big kind hearts, Are fixing up my broken parts. Because of you, I feel so bright. You fill my days with hope and light. So, here's a thank you, just for you, For everything you say and do. Keep helping me, keep being kind, Together, happiness we'll find.

Planting Activity: Give each child a small plant or seeds to plant in a cup or where lands available take the learners outside to plant tree(s) or seeds. Explain how plants help the environment by cleaning the air and providing food and shelter for animals.

Teacher will wrap up the unit by telling the learners that we can help our plant and animal friends by not littering, planting more trees and flowers, and making sure we don't waste water. When we take care of nature, we are helping all the plants and animals that live there! Adding that we should remember, plants and animals are always helping each other out. And we can help them too by taking care of the environment where they live.

Revisit the Essential Question: Teacher will say: 'Everyone repeat after me' 'All the things that I have talked about, seen, and discovered tells me...' (Pointing to the words on the chart in the classroom) 'How do plants and animals make a difference to me and my world?'

inclusive_learning_strategies:
Observation Corner- Who will survive?
What did you notice over the two days you looked at the plants?
How do the plants look?
Why are they looking like that?
Which one survived?
Why did the other one die or shriveled up?
What do plants need to survive?
Language Arts, Math and Science: Plant Shop
Plant Shop

This ILS provides many opportunities for the teacher to enhance the children’s development of LA skills through Shared Reading. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions.

Set up a pretend plant shop with different sections labeled "Sun-loving Plants" (cacti, aloes, hibiscus...) and "Shade-loving Plants" (ferns, Hosta...). Real plants are best but in cases where they are not available use pictures or toy plants.
The teacher dresses and acts like a florist, explaining to the learners what is needed by all plants and what some plants need, and others may not need to survive.
Learners are given opportunities to ask questions and make comments.

The teacher points learners’ attention to the signs in the shop (Sun loving plants, Shade loving plants) and guides the learners to read the signs by decoding the words. Learners will also study a chart that the teacher has made, with a list of items and cost. The chart will include items a plant will need to survive, such as water, shade, sunlight, soil nutrients).
Learners will work in pairs to identify a plant and "buy" the items they think their plant needs (sunlight, water, shade, air, soil nutrients, etc.). The teacher clarifies accordingly.

In reading the chart and buying the items at the plant shop, learners will name monies and identify quantities in cents. (E.g. 1 shade cost 5 cents and 1 sunlight costs 3 cents then 5 cents and 3 cents make 8 cents; 5+3=8). They will also count objects. This should all be done orally, with the teacher’s support.

The teacher will invite children to take part in role play with the Plant Shop at various times, when the children have the opportunity to choose various activities (such as drawing, puzzles, building).

The teacher will help the children count the number of animals and plants in each group. To make sure the children understand the graph, the teacher can ask a few children some questions, such as: how many plants and animals live in the water?

inclusive_assessment_strategies: The teacher will enter information into the checklist for Operations with Numbers Outcome 2.1.1 based on observation and conversation with the learners.
